case_name: MTBE removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for MTBE-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws raw water from a river-influenced reservoir and has detected MTBE after recurring upstream fuel-related runoff incidents. The plant needs a reliable treatment approach to control MTBE-related taste, odor, and health concerns while continuing to produce drinking water. The utility prefers proven technologies that can be integrated with conventional treatment operations and does not want to adopt unnecessarily complex high-energy systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong MTBE reduction suitable for drinking water production and minimize taste and odor breakthrough under normal operating conditions.
secondary_goals: use established and field-proven treatment technology
preserve compatibility with existing conventional treatment infrastructure

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: spent carbon replacement or regeneration is manageable; utility prefers to avoid difficult concentrate disposal
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable treatment performance is required despite typical seasonal surface water quality variation

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: remove suspended solids and reduce particulate/organic loading ahead of adsorption
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb MTBE and provide a proven polishing barrier for dissolved organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.post_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: GAC is a widely applied and well-established technology for removal of taste-and-odor causing and gasoline-related organic contaminants in drinking water treatment
the utility prefers proven technology and GAC can be integrated downstream of conventional surface water treatment
single-contaminant control with moderate cost and moderate operating complexity aligns well with an adsorption-based polishing approach
surface water DOC is moderate, so pretreatment plus well-managed GAC can provide a practical and familiar solution
potential_risks: MTBE is more weakly adsorbed than many other hydrophobic organics, so empty bed contact time and carbon changeout frequency must be managed carefully
natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten bed life
breakthrough monitoring is needed to prevent taste and odor complaints

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: reduce solids and stabilize water prior to volatile contaminant removal
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_packed_tower_aeration: transfer volatile MTBE from water to air
core_unit_functions.off_gas_control_if_required: control released volatile contaminants where air emissions management is needed

applicability_rationale: MTBE is a volatile organic compound and air stripping is an established removal method
the technology is proven and can be effective where transfer of the contaminant to the gas phase is acceptable
it can be considered when the utility is comfortable with aeration equipment and potential air handling requirements
potential_risks: MTBE is less amenable to stripping than more volatile gasoline compounds, so towers may require higher air-to-water ratios or greater packing depth
off-gas emissions may trigger additional treatment or permitting requirements
performance can be affected by temperature and hydraulic variability

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: reduce turbidity and fouling potential prior to oxidation
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_uv_hydrogen_p: generate hydroxyl radicals to degrade MTBE
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration_or_GAC_po: remove oxidation byproducts and provide final polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is a recognized treatment option for difficult dissolved organic contaminants including MTBE
it is technically capable when direct adsorption or stripping alone is insufficient
post-polishing is appropriate to manage partial oxidation byproducts and provide additional finished water protection
potential_risks: higher energy and chemical demand than adsorption-based treatment
incomplete oxidation can form intermediate compounds requiring downstream polishing
UV transmittance and oxidant dose control are important for reliable performance

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: provide initial solids handling and process stabilization
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide episodic adsorption of dissolved MTBE in the conventional treatment train
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: capture PAC and suspended solids for removal
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove remaining particles and PAC carryover

applicability_rationale: PAC is a familiar and proven option for short-term or intermittent organic contaminant control in conventional plants
this approach can be attractive when contamination events are episodic rather than constant
integration with existing surface water treatment is straightforward
potential_risks: PAC is generally less reliable than fixed-bed GAC for sustained MTBE removal
high PAC doses may be required because MTBE adsorption is relatively weak
additional sludge production and solids handling are expected

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide a physical barrier for broad dissolved contaminant reduction
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.post_disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in distributed water

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment can be technically considered where broad-spectrum contaminant control is desired
it may be chosen if other dissolved contaminants also become important in the future
potential_risks: high capital and operating costs compared with more conventional MTBE treatment options
concentrate management is burdensome for a utility seeking straightforward proven treatment
this is unnecessarily complex for a single-contaminant MTBE case under moderate constraints

preferred_rank_pattern: GAC-based treatment integrated with conventional surface water pretreatment should rank first
air stripping should rank near the top as a proven alternative but not above GAC under stated utility preferences
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first for this single-contaminant MTBE case

must_reflect: compatibility with conventional drinking water plant operation
avoidance of unnecessarily complex high-energy solutions

must_contain: why GAC is appropriate as a proven polishing technology for MTBE in drinking water treatment
why air stripping is feasible but may need air emissions management and can be less attractive operationally
why advanced oxidation is technically capable but ranks below simpler proven options
why membrane treatment ranks lower under moderate cost and energy constraints

should_mention: MTBE can be harder to adsorb than some other petroleum hydrocarbons
competition from natural organic matter can reduce carbon bed life
air stripping may require off-gas treatment or regulatory review
advanced oxidation may need downstream polishing for byproducts
